summary:
Acromegaly develops due to hormonal imbalances affecting adults whose bones have already stopped growing; thus, it cannot occur before closure of the epiphyses as described by options involving premature or concurrent conditions related to cartilage ossification sites fusing together early in life stages preceding adulthood onset symptoms like those seen clinically associated with this endocrine disorder characterized primarily through excessive GH/IGF-1 secretion causing skeletal enlargement among other features such as facial changes etc...
To summarize more concisely while still capturing essential information for clarity without redundancy: 
Acromegaly arises exclusively once individuals reach their full height because they've ceased longitudinal bone growth at puberty; hence its occurrence is linked strictly to an age past completion of epiphyseal plate closures rather than any earlier developmental stage where continued linear limb elongation might be impacted instead resulting from abnormal pituitary gland function later in life impacting somatic tissues including skull base structures giving rise distinctive physical appearance alterations alongside joint pain muscle weakness fatigue amongst others indicative signs pointing towards diagnosis via biochemical markers imaging studies confirming elevated IGF levels along with radiographic evidence showing enlarged sella turcica consistent findings supporting correct identification treatment planning management strategies tailored accordingly based upon individual patient needs severity course progression factors considered comprehensively throughout
